sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: RIGHT.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 2, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: - Level: 1 - Objects: - A horizontal assembly: light blue square (left), gray bar, light blue square (right). - A gray square with purple dots (bottom left). - A gray square with a 3x3 grid of white/gray squares (bottom center). - A green bar on the right (HUD/timer). - Goal: Unknown.
- Action model: - MOUSE (row, col): Click a specific cell. - UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT: Move something or the cursor.
- Recent findings: - Initial state.
- Open questions: - What is the goal? - How do the objects interact? - Is there a cursor I can move?
- Plan: Explore the board and test interactions. I'll start by inspecting the segmentation to identify the objects' positions and then try clicking them or using directional keys. 1. Inspect segmentation to find object coordinates. 2. Try clicking the objects to see if they react. 3. Try moving with arrow keys. Let's start by inspecting.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 1 of about 36 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
The `RIGHT` action did not change the board (`board_changed: false`). This suggests that either the `RIGHT` action does nothing in this state, or it moves something that isn't visually represented or is blocked.
Let's re-examine the objects and their positions from the first turn's segmentation.
- Object 2: light blue square at [17, 12] to [22, 16] (approximately)
- Object 3: light blue square inside object 2? Wait, boundary [[18, 13], [18, 16], [21, 16], [21, 13]]
- Object 4: gray bar at [19, 17] to [22, 38]
- Object 5: light blue square at [19, 39] to [22, 40]
- Object 6: light blue square at [19, 41] to [22, 42]
- Object 7: gray square at [47, 22] to [63, 38] (contains white/gray grid)
- Object 11: gray square at [50, 11] to [59, 20] (contains purple dots)
Wait, looking at the image:
- There's a light blue block on the left.
- A gray bar extending from it to the right.
- Another light blue block on the right end of the bar.
- Below, there's a gray square with purple dots on the left.
- A gray square with a 3x3 grid of white/gray squares on the right.
The `RIGHT` action didn't do anything. Let's try `MOUSE` clicks on the objects to see if they are interactive.
Let's try clicking the light blue block on the left, the gray bar, the light blue block on the right, the purple dot square, and the grid square.
Wait, maybe the goal is to move the light blue block or the bar? Or maybe the grid is a puzzle to solve?
The purple dots square looks like a "key" or "pattern" and the grid looks like a "lock" or "input".
Let's try clicking the grid cells.
Grid cells (approximate from segmentation):
Object 8: [49, 24] to [51, 26] (top-left)
Object 9: [49, 29] to [51, 31] (top-center)
Object 10: [49, 34] to [51, 36] (top-right)
Object 16: [54, 24] to [56, 26] (mid-left)
Object 17: [54, 29] to [56, 31] (mid-center)
Object 18: [54, 34] to [56, 36] (mid-right)
Object 20: [59, 24] to [61, 26] (bot-left)
Object 21: [59, 29] to [61, 31] (bot-center)
Object 22: [59, 34] to [61, 36] (bot-right)
Let's try clicking one of these.
Wait, look at the purple dots square (Object 11/12/13/14/15/19).
It has purple dots at:
- [51, 15] to [52, 16] (top-centerish)
- [54, 12] to [55, 13] (mid-left)
- [54, 18] to [55, 19] (mid-right)
- [57, 15] to [58, 16] (bot-centerish)
This looks like a diamond pattern of purple dots.
Maybe I need to replicate this pattern on the 3x3 grid?
Pattern:
- Top-center: Yes
- Mid-left: Yes
- Mid-center: No
- Mid-right: Yes
- Bot-center: Yes
- Top-left: No
- Top-right: No
- Bot-left: No
- Bot-right: No
Let's try clicking the cells in the grid to match this pattern.
Top-center: Object 9 [49, 29] to [51, 31] -> click (50, 30)
Mid-left: Object 16 [54, 24] to [56, 26] -> click (55, 25)
Mid-right: Object 18 [54, 34] to [56, 36] -> click (55, 35)
Bot-center: Object 21 [59, 29] to [61, 31] -> click (60, 30)
Let's try clicking one cell first to see if it changes color.
